Personally, I think Robert Redford's voice surpasses that of George Clooney in terms of its weight & appeal to my ear. Redford had a certain richness in his tone that rivets my attention when I watch his movies. Clooney, not so much, either as a young man or now.
Gregory Peck had a deep, mellow voice. Resonant & powerful, dignified.
Al Pacino's voice is one of cinema's most distinctive, ranging from a whisper to a roar. Some say Pacino's voice was whiny & nasal, yet clean in his early movies. Then emerged his trademark raspy voice in later films, courtesy of a 4-pack-per-day smoking habit in the '80s.
I like Jude Law's voice, warm & expressive, with a pleasing softness. 
Antonio Banderas has an appealing, sexy voice. I like his (European, not Latin-American) Spanish accent.
